name: tangerine mint vinaigrette
instructions:
Mix tangerine juice, a small handful of mint leaves, a splash of vinegar (white or white wine), and a splash of olive oil in a glass. Heavily skewed toward tangerine juice and mint; only splashes of the vinegar and oil.

INFUSION STEP: let the mixture sit at least 10 minutes for the mint to infuse. Optionally blend at this stage — see notes for the diminishing-returns caveat. Strain out the mint.

FOR THE SALAD ITSELF: separately, take fresh mint leaves off the stems, chiffonade FINE, and fold into the salad as a leaf component. The dressing's infused mint flavor is not enough on its own — the salad wants the chiffonaded fresh leaves on top of it. Don't skip this step.
notes:
Standard form uses tangerine juice for the sweet citrus component. Vinegar (white or white wine) and olive oil are splashes only — the dish is about the tangerine and mint, not the vinaigrette mechanics.

BLENDING THE MINT (DIMINISHING RETURNS): blending the mint extracts a bit more flavor than a standing infusion, but not enough to skip the chiffonade-the-leaves-into-the-salad step. Blender + strained infusion alone leaves the salad under-minty. The chiffonaded leaves on the salad itself are doing real work.

CHIFFONADE TECHNIQUE (FOR THE SALAD MINT): pick the leaves off the stems before chiffonading — do NOT include the stems, which are bitter. Chiffonade fine; bruising large leaves with a coarse cut releases bitter compounds. Skip any large, mishandled, or crushed leaves that are starting to brown. The finely-cut clean leaves are what carry the dish.

LEMON ALTERNATIVE (REQUIRES SUGAR): lemon juice and lemon zest can replace the tangerine, but lemon doesn't bring tangerine's natural sweetness. To match the canonical version with lemon, you need to add external sugar — mint specifically wants something sweet to play against. Lemon-and-sugar gets you closer to the tangerine register but it's not quite the same; the tangerine's combined sweet-and-citrus is the easier path.
